text:
Theories of intraparty competition typically focus on external electoral conditions and formal internal rules. This article moves beyond this "black box" by analyzing the substantive expression of preferences within parties. We argue that while electoral losses drive an increase in intraparty preference heterogeneity, the internal electoral system penalizes candidates who deviate from the party's ideological median. Furthermore, we find that party members grant leaders significantly more leeway to manage competing policy demands when the party is in government compared to when it is in opposition.

To test these propositions, we introduce a new database of party congress speeches from Germany and France, utilizing automated text classification to estimate the relative ideological positions of speakers. The results demonstrate that speeches delivered at party meetings provide empirical insights into the internal preference distributions and power sticks of political actors. Our findings reveal a nuanced relationship between governing status, economic performance, and the degree of intraparty disagreement, highlighting how the institutional context reshapes internal dissent.
